clean_story:
Moving through time. Breaking its arrow.

The term time travel merges two simple ideas: shifting your place in time, as you might walk from one room to another. But time isn’t a hallway—it’s more like a river, always flowing forward. Or so it seems.

Einstein’s equations in 1905 showed time stretches and bends, like taffy pulled by speed or gravity. A astronaut near a black hole ages slower than their twin on Earth. This isn’t fiction: GPS satellites adjust for time slowing 38 microseconds daily due to their high-speed orbit. Yet this is time dilation—a one-way drift, not a round trip.

True time travel demands loops. In 1949, mathematician Kurt Gödel found a solution where the universe spins, dragging time into circles. Ride this cosmic whirlpool, and you could return to your own past—like walking a spiral staircase and ending up where you started. No machine needed, just extreme physics. The catch? Such a universe would tear itself apart. His model required negative energy, a substance we’ve never found.

Closer to home, wormholes—tunnels through spacetime—offer theoretical shortcuts. In 1988, physicist Kip Thorne calculated that a wormhole’s mouth could be moved near light-speed, turning it into a time machine. Step through one end in 2050, exit the other in 2024. But wormholes collapse instantly unless held open by exotic matter (with negative energy again). Lab experiments have simulated microscopic wormholes using quantum entanglement, but they vanish in trillionths of a second.

Paradoxes lurk. The grandfather paradox asks: What if you killed your grandfather before your parent was born? Most theories dodge this with self-consistency: the past resists change. Try to hand Shakespeare a smartphone, and the battery dies, or he burns it as heresy. The universe enforces its own rules.

Three related subjects are closed timelike curves, quantum eraser experiments, and how memory distorts time.
